title: Make drain mode default and stop wasting no_work runs
description:
## Problem
Orca currently continues running after queue exhaustion when agents emit `result=no_work` with `loop_action=continue`, causing avoidable token burn.

## Decision intent
Drain mode should be the default operating behavior: once there is no actionable work, the loop should stop unless there is an explicit reason to retry.

## Scope
- Make drain semantics the default in loop policy.
- Define deterministic stop behavior for `no_work` that minimizes false stops from transient states/races.
- Preserve an explicit operator-controlled override for watch/poll mode when desired.
- Ensure behavior is deterministic in both bounded (`--runs N`) and unbounded runs.

## Acceptance criteria
- In default mode, sustained queue exhaustion leads to loop stop without repeated `no_work` token burn.
- Transient `no_work` cases (e.g. race/refresh window) can be retried in a controlled way.
- Behavior is documented in README and operator guide.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented drain-by-default no_work policy in harness: added ORCA_NO_WORK_DRAIN_MODE (drain|watch), ORCA_NO_WORK_RETRY_LIMIT (default 1), start/orca CLI flags (--drain/--watch/--no-work-retries), deterministic consecutive no_work stop handling in agent-loop.sh, and README/OPERATOR_GUIDE updates.

title: Reconcile existing mode/approach roadmap with assignment-first planner architecture
description:
Problem
- Existing open tasks (e.g. mode selector / approach injection) were scoped before the assignment-first concurrency decision.

Scope
- Review open roadmap tasks for overlap/conflict with assignment/planner work.
- Update dependencies, priorities, and descriptions to reflect new default architecture.
- Close or supersede obsolete tasks with explicit rationale.

Acceptance Criteria
- Queue reflects one coherent roadmap without contradictory defaults.
- Superseded tasks are documented (close reason or linked follow-up).
- Operator guide sequencing matches the updated plan.

title: Add assigned-issue execution contract to Orca runs
description:
Problem
- Agents currently self-select issues, which increases claim races and overlap risk in multi-agent runs.

Decision
- Move to assigned work by default. Each launched agent receives exactly one assigned issue for the run.

Scope
- Add assigned issue env/plumbing (e.g. ORCA_ASSIGNED_ISSUE_ID) from launcher to agent-loop/prompt.
- Update AGENT_PROMPT contract to require working the assigned issue only (or explicit no_work/blocked path).
- Add loop-side validation that summary.issue_id matches assigned issue when assignment is set.

Acceptance Criteria
- When assignment is set, agent run completes only that issue or returns explicit blocked/no_work result.
- Summary/metrics capture assigned issue id and actual issue id for auditability.
- Existing non-assigned mode remains available only as an explicit override for recovery/debugging.

comments.author: agent-2
comments.text: Implemented assigned-issue execution contract end-to-end: start.sh now defaults to assigned mode and injects ORCA_ASSIGNED_ISSUE_ID/ORCA_ASSIGNMENT_MODE, agent-loop enforces summary.issue_id match when assignment is set, and metrics/summary now include assigned vs actual issue audit fields. Updated AGENT_PROMPT + README contract/docs. Validation: bash tests/regression_assigned_issue_contract.sh; bash tests/regression_drain_stop_observability.sh.

title: Add orca wait command for non-interactive completion monitoring
description:
Problem
- Agent-driven Orca usage currently requires manual polling/monitoring of tmux sessions and text status output.
- There is no first-class blocking primitive to wait for run completion and return machine-usable success/failure semantics.

Why this matters
- Higher-level agents/orchestrators need a deterministic way to launch Orca, wait for completion, and branch on outcome without human supervision.

Scope
- Add a new command: orca wait.
- Implement blocking wait semantics for Orca sessions with scope controls:
  1. exact session id filtering
  2. session prefix filtering
  3. default scope (all Orca sessions)
- Add timeout support and deterministic polling behavior.
- Return deterministic exit codes for automation, for example:
  - success (all scoped runs finished without hard failure)
  - timeout
  - scoped run failure detected
  - invalid usage/config
- Emit concise final rollup on completion; include optional JSON output for machine consumers.
- Ensure race-safe completion detection when session exits and artifacts/metrics finalize near-simultaneously.

Implementation Notes / Potential Issues
- Avoid relying only on tmux session presence; account for short race windows where session exits before metrics flush is observed.
- Reuse existing session scoping model from status where possible.
- Keep polling lightweight; do not repeatedly full-scan large metrics files on every loop tick.
- Define behavior when no scoped sessions exist at invocation time (immediate return vs error) and document it explicitly.

Acceptance Criteria
- Agents can run: start -> wait -> inspect exit code, with no manual status watching.
- wait supports timeout and session scoping.
- Exit codes are documented and stable.
- Optional JSON result is parseable and includes terminal reason + scoped summary counts.
- Docs include at least one non-interactive orchestration example.

title: Improve status performance by avoiding full metrics rescans
description:
## Problem
`status.sh` repeatedly scans/parses full `metrics.jsonl`, which scales poorly and slows frequent polling.

## Scope
- Reduce status runtime cost by avoiding full-file parsing on every call (e.g. tail-window parsing and/or incremental summary cache).
- Keep output correctness for key counters and latest-run signals.
- Add a simple runtime benchmark/check target to prevent regressions.

## Acceptance criteria
- Typical status latency is materially reduced on larger metrics files.
- Key health/activity fields remain correct.
- Performance approach and limits are documented.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented status full-mode metrics caching keyed by metrics.jsonl size+mtime, replaced repeated full-file jq scans with one cached summary build, documented cache behavior/limits in README, and added tests/status_metrics_perf_check.sh. Validation: bash -n status.sh, bash tests/status_metrics_perf_check.sh, bash tests/regression_drain_stop_observability.sh, status.sh quick/full.

title: Add assignment/concurrency observability and regression tests
description:
Problem
- Without dedicated telemetry/tests, assignment and contention controls may regress silently.

Scope
- Add metrics fields for planned_assigned_issue, assignment_source, and assignment_outcome (matched/mismatch/unassigned).
- Record planner decisions in an artifact/log for post-run analysis.
- Add regression tests for:
  - claim-race reduction under assignment mode
  - contention-key conflict avoidance
  - px:exclusive batch behavior

Acceptance Criteria
- New observability fields are present and documented.
- Tests cover planner + start integration paths and pass in CI/local harness checks.
- Status/metrics tooling remains backward compatible.

title: Add Ubuntu/WSL preflight doctor command for Orca setup readiness
description:
Problem
- Orca currently relies on implicit environment assumptions. Operators discover missing prerequisites only at runtime (e.g. missing binaries, invalid queue workspace, missing git identity), which causes wasted runs and avoidable recovery work.

Context
- Target platform for this onboarding flow is Ubuntu on WSL.
- Agent command is fixed to Codex for now, but the check architecture should allow future command configurability.
- Existing startup checks in `start.sh` are useful but happen too late and are not exposed as a first-class onboarding command.

Scope
- Add `orca doctor` command (read-only; no side effects).
- Implement a dedicated checker script (e.g. `doctor.sh`) with both human-readable and `--json` output.
- Check at minimum:
  1. platform is WSL/Ubuntu (or provide explicit unsupported warning)
  2. required binaries exist: `git`, `tmux`, `jq`, `flock`, `br`, `codex`
  3. repo context is valid (`git rev-parse`, `origin` remote present)
  4. local git identity exists (`git config --local user.name` and `user.email`)
  5. queue workspace health (`.beads/` exists, `br doctor` passes, id prefix present)
  6. helper scripts exist and are executable (`with-lock.sh`, `queue-write-main.sh`, `merge-main.sh`)
- Provide actionable remediation hints per failed check (exact commands).
- Return non-zero exit code when any hard requirement fails.

Potential Issues / Edge Cases to Handle
- WSL detection can be brittle; avoid hardcoding one heuristic only.
- Some checks may require network/auth and be flaky; distinguish local prerequisite failures vs remote reachability failures.
- JSON output must remain stable for future automation.

Acceptance Criteria
- `./orca.sh doctor` exists and reports pass/fail per check with remediation hints.
- `./orca.sh doctor --json` emits machine-readable structured results.
- Exit code is non-zero when hard checks fail.
- Command is idempotent and does not mutate repo or queue state.
- README and operator guide include basic doctor usage.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text:
Doctor output contract guidance:
- Emit stable check IDs in JSON output (for example platform.wsl_ubuntu, dep.br.present, queue.br_doctor, agent.codex.auth) so automation can key on IDs instead of message text.
- Include severity per check (error or warn) and make process exit code depend on error checks only.
- Keep remediation fields structured (for example remediation.commands array) to reduce parser fragility.

title: Document and regression-test doctor/bootstrap onboarding flow
description:
Problem
- New setup commands will regress without explicit docs and regression checks.

Scope
- Add onboarding documentation (e.g. `docs/setup.md`) covering:
  1. Ubuntu/WSL prerequisites
  2. recommended flow: `orca doctor` -> `orca bootstrap` -> `orca doctor`
  3. manual steps (Codex auth, git auth/push, shell PATH refresh)
  4. troubleshooting mapped to common doctor/bootstrap failures
- Update `README.md` and `OPERATOR_GUIDE.md` to reference the setup flow.
- Add regression tests for doctor/bootstrap behavior:
  - parseable JSON contract for doctor output
  - non-zero exit on failing hard checks
  - bootstrap dry-run output sanity
  - at least one fixture-based test to ensure remediation hints remain present

Potential Issues / Edge Cases to Handle
- Tests must avoid mutating host machine state; rely on dry-run/fixture strategy where possible.
- Keep docs aligned with the exact `br` install approach chosen in bootstrap.

Acceptance Criteria
- A new operator can follow docs and reach a healthy baseline without ad-hoc tribal knowledge.
- Regression tests fail when doctor/bootstrap output contracts break.
- Core docs and help text are consistent with implemented behavior.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text:
Test/docs alignment guidance:
- Add regression coverage that asserts doctor JSON check IDs remain stable over time.
- Add regression coverage that asserts bootstrap surfaces a deterministic fail-hard auth message when Codex auth is missing.
- Document the pinned/verified br install strategy clearly in setup docs to avoid drift from implementation.

title: Add start-time mode selector (execute|explore) with explicit override semantics
description:
Problem
- Operators currently hand-tune knobs each session. There is no first-class way to select a throughput-biased vs autonomy-biased profile.

Scope
- Add mode selector (flag or env) for start.sh profile defaults:
  - execute: throughput-biased defaults
  - explore: autonomy/discovery-biased defaults
- Keep all existing explicit knobs as higher-precedence overrides.
- Log resolved mode and effective key runtime settings at startup.

Acceptance Criteria
- Selecting mode changes only default values, not hard behavior contracts.
- Explicit CLI/env settings override mode defaults deterministically.
- README/OPERATOR_GUIDE document precedence and examples.

Notes
- Avoid runtime task classification and avoid adding policy logic in harness.

title: Define issue parallel-safety metadata (labels) and operator guidance
description:
Problem
- Orca lacks explicit issue metadata for parallel safety, so agents can collide on overlapping files/areas.

Decision
- Add lightweight label-based metadata first (no schema migration):
  - px:exclusive   -> must run alone
  - ck:<key>       -> contention key (issues sharing key should not run together)

Scope
- Document label taxonomy and examples in README/OPERATOR_GUIDE.
- Document default behavior: unlabeled issues are parallel-allowed.
- Add authoring guidance for new issues (when to mark exclusive / contention keys).

Acceptance Criteria
- Taxonomy is documented with concrete examples.
- Clear precedence rules are documented (px:exclusive overrides ck:*).
- No harness behavior change in this task; this is metadata contract + docs only.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Completed: documented parallel-safety label taxonomy (px:exclusive, ck:<key>) in README and OPERATOR_GUIDE, including precedence and authoring guidance. No harness behavior changes in this task.

title: Align docs with local-main policy and runtime knob behavior
description:
## Problem
Core docs should reflect runtime behavior and local source-of-truth policy.

## Scope
- Update docs to codify local `main` as default source-of-truth for local Orca runs (including how divergence from `origin/main` is handled).
- Ensure runtime knob docs match implemented behavior for `ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO` and `ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH` injection.
- Cross-check for consistency across core operator docs (`README.md`, `OPERATOR_GUIDE.md`, and any directly related references).

## Acceptance criteria
- A new reader can infer local `main` default behavior unambiguously.
- Runtime knob documentation matches actual `start.sh`/`agent-loop.sh` behavior.
- No conflicting statements remain in the updated docs set.
close_reason: Docs now align with local-main policy and runtime knob behavior

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Updated scope per operator direction: remove Option C/decision-log-based evaluation content from tasking; keep docs coherence focused on runtime behavior and local-main policy.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Updated docs to codify local main as default local source-of-truth (with explicit divergence behavior vs origin/main), and aligned runtime knob docs for ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO/ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH defaults, validation, and injection behavior with start.sh + agent-loop.sh.

title: Integrate planner into start flow and make assignment default
description:
Problem
- Planner output must drive actual launches; otherwise assignment remains advisory and races persist.

Scope
- Update start/orca command flow so launch uses planner output by default.
- Pass assigned issue id per agent session to agent-loop.
- If planner yields fewer assignments than requested count, launch only assigned sessions and report why.
- Provide explicit escape hatch to disable assignment mode for recovery/debugging.

Acceptance Criteria
- Default start behavior launches assigned runs (not free-pick runs).
- Launch logs include planned assignments and skipped reasons.
- Existing queue/merge invariants remain intact.

title: Add safe run-branch garbage collection command
description:
## Problem
Run branches (`swarm/*-run-*`) accumulate over time and create local ref clutter.

## Scope
- Add a safe garbage-collection command/script for stale run branches.
- Only prune branches that are merged/obsolete and not active in any worktree/session.
- Provide dry-run mode and clear reporting.

## Acceptance criteria
- Operators can safely clean historical run branches without impacting active work.
- Cleanup command has dry-run and explicit safety checks.
- Docs include when/how to run cleanup.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented gc-run-branches command with dry-run default and safety guards: only prune swarm/*-run-* when merged into main and not active in worktree/tmux session. Updated orca.sh dispatch + README/OPERATOR_GUIDE docs. Validation: bash -n orca.sh gc-run-branches.sh and dry-run invocation in this worktree.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Completed and merged to main. Added ./orca.sh gc-run-branches command with dry-run default, merged-into-main filter, active worktree/session protection, and clear reporting; updated README and OPERATOR_GUIDE usage/docs.

title: Run execute/explore + approach experiments and decide default posture
description:
Problem
- Orca lacks evidence for which mode/approach combinations improve outcomes for different session intents.

Scope
- Run comparative sessions across execute/explore (and optionally approach snippets) on similar issue sets.
- Evaluate with attributed metrics and qualitative run summaries.
- Produce decision note: keep, tune, or remove defaults/snippets.

Acceptance Criteria
- At least 10 comparable issue runs per profile before conclusions.
- Report includes: no_work rate/tokens, result distribution, follow-up/rework signals, operator intervention count.
- Decision and rationale recorded in docs/decision-log.md.

Notes
- Do not optimize for checklist compliance; optimize for outcome quality and cost.

title: Fix status loop action reporting from metrics summary
description:
## Problem
`status.sh` displays per-agent `loop=n/a` even when runs produced loop actions, because it reads `.loop_action` at the top level of metrics rows while loop data is stored under `.summary.loop_action`.

## Scope
- Update `status.sh` parsing for agent activity rows to read:
  - `.summary.loop_action`
  - `.summary.loop_action_reason`
- Keep backward compatibility if old rows used top-level fields (optional fallback).
- Validate output formatting still works when loop fields are missing.

## Acceptance criteria
- `orca status` shows actual loop action and reason for new metrics rows.
- Existing output remains readable when loop fields are absent.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented in status.sh: agent activity now reads loop_action/loop_action_reason from summary.* first with fallback to legacy top-level fields. Validated with synthetic metrics rows covering summary-only, legacy-only, and missing loop fields.

title: Add status run-in-progress indicator and session scoping
description:
## Problem
During long runs, status can appear stale until metrics are appended. Also old sessions can pollute current view.

## Scope
- Add a run-in-progress indicator per active agent/session.
- Add session-scoping/filter options for status views (e.g. current session prefix or explicit session id).
- Keep backward-compatible defaults for existing operator workflows.

## Acceptance criteria
- Operators can tell if an agent is actively running vs idle/stalled.
- Status can focus on relevant current sessions without historical noise.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented status run-in-progress detection from active session artifacts; added --session-id/--session-prefix scoping for quick/full metrics+activity views; updated README/OPERATOR_GUIDE; added tests/status_session_scope_and_progress.sh (pass). Existing performance check tests/status_metrics_perf_check.sh is timing-sensitive and may fail under load.

title: Add optional work-approach prompt snippet injection
description:
Problem
- Orca currently has one coordination prompt with no lightweight mechanism to test alternate work approaches across sessions.

Scope
- Add __WORK_APPROACH__ placeholder support in AGENT_PROMPT.md rendering.
- Add ORCA_WORK_APPROACH_FILE env knob; when unset, behavior remains unchanged.
- Add starter approach snippets for code-focused and docs-focused sessions under approaches/.

Acceptance Criteria
- With ORCA_WORK_APPROACH_FILE unset: prompt output is unchanged from current behavior.
- With ORCA_WORK_APPROACH_FILE set: snippet content is injected into rendered prompt.
- README documents usage and warns that guidance is advisory.

Notes
- This is an experiment enabler, not policy enforcement.

title: Add mode/approach attribution to run metrics and session logs
description:
Problem
- Mode and prompt-approach experiments are not attributable in current artifacts, so outcome comparisons are weak.

Scope
- Add mode identifier and approach metadata to run/session observability:
  - mode id (e.g. execute|explore|custom)
  - approach source identifier/path (when set)
  - approach content hash (SHA256)
- Emit fields in metrics.jsonl and startup/session logs.

Acceptance Criteria
- New fields are present for every run (empty/none when unset).
- Existing metrics parsers/status output continue to work.
- README docs runtime knobs/fields are updated.

Notes
- This task is prerequisite for trustworthy A/B evaluation of mode and approach guidance.

comments.author: agent-2
comments.text: Implemented mode/approach attribution in run observability: added ORCA_MODE_ID + ORCA_WORK_APPROACH_FILE wiring, emitted mode_id/approach_source/approach_sha256 in metrics.jsonl, logged attribution in session/run logs, updated README runtime knobs + metrics fields, and extended regression_drain_stop_observability to assert default-null attribution fields. Validated with tests/regression_drain_stop_observability.sh and tests/status_session_scope_and_progress.sh.

title: Fix drain-stop loop-action observability ordering
description:
## Problem
Drain-stop observability is inconsistent: in `agent-loop.sh`, run observability artifacts are finalized before no-work drain-stop logic mutates loop action/reason, so metrics/summary can miss the actual stop reason.

## Scope
- Ensure final recorded run artifacts (metrics row + summary markdown) reflect post-decision loop action/reason for drain-stop exits.
- Keep deterministic ordering so stop reason is visible in both machine-readable and human-readable artifacts.
- Add a regression check covering a no-work drain stop path.

## Acceptance criteria
- For a run that triggers drain stop, metrics and summary both include `loop_action=stop` and the drain stop reason.
- No regression for normal completed/blocked/no_work (non-stop) runs.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented fix in agent-loop: apply no_work drain-stop loop_action/reason before finalize_run_observability so summary.md and metrics.jsonl capture final stop reason. Added regression script tests/regression_drain_stop_observability.sh and validated it passes.

title: Cap agent launches to queue depth at start time (with override)
description:
Problem
- Fixed launch count causes avoidable no_work runs and token burn when ready queue depth is below requested agent count.

Scope
- In start.sh, cap new launches to available ready work by default.
- Add ORCA_FORCE_COUNT=1 escape hatch to bypass capping.
- Log requested/running/ready/launched counts for auditability.

Acceptance Criteria
- Starting with count > ready issues launches only necessary new sessions (unless ORCA_FORCE_COUNT=1).
- Existing idempotent behavior for already-running sessions remains intact.
- README documents behavior and override knob.

Notes
- Keep this deterministic and transport-level; no runtime issue classification.
close_reason: Completed: start.sh now caps new launches to ready queue depth by default, supports ORCA_FORCE_COUNT=1 override, logs requested/running/ready/launched counts, and README is updated.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented in start.sh + README: default launch capping now limits new launches to current ready queue depth (br ready --json), added ORCA_FORCE_COUNT=1 override, and added launch planning/summary logs with requested/running/ready/launch counts. Validation run: bash -n start.sh; guard path check confirmed ORCA_FORCE_COUNT=2 exits with validation error.

title: Add lightweight protocol telemetry for Option C manual reversibility
description:
## Problem
Option C is intentionally autonomy-first and reversible by operator decision, but current telemetry is weak for manual reversal decisions (e.g., protocol drift/race trends).

## Scope
Add lightweight, non-enforcing telemetry to improve manual review quality:
- Capture explicit counters/events for:
  - claim publication race/failure events
  - queue helper failures
  - merge helper failures (including `.beads` rejection)
  - lock timeout/contention events
- Emit machine-readable data (metrics JSONL and/or dedicated events JSONL).
- Surface a concise rollup in `status.sh` for recent window.

## Constraints
- Do not add hard runtime enforcement.
- Keep implementation cheap and deterministic.

## Acceptance criteria
- Operator can answer “is protocol drift/race overhead increasing?” from artifacts without log spelunking.
- Status output includes a compact recent telemetry summary.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text: Operator decision: telemetry for Option C evaluation will be tracked manually outside tasking. Closing this issue as out-of-scope for implementation.

title: Clarify --runs as max upper bound with early-stop precedence
description:
## Problem
`--runs` semantics are ambiguous in docs/mental model. It should represent a hard upper bound, not an instruction to spend runs when there is no work.

## Scope
- Define `--runs` as max iterations (upper bound), always subject to earlier stop conditions (drain/no_work, explicit stop, failure policy).
- Update command help, README, and operator guide wording to remove ambiguity.
- Add regression checks for early termination before max run count.

## Acceptance criteria
- Running with `--runs N` never forces extra `no_work` iterations after stop conditions are met.
- Docs and help text consistently describe `--runs` as an upper bound.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented: clarified --runs as a max upper bound in start/loop messaging and docs (README, OPERATOR_GUIDE); added regression assertion that drain-stop exits before MAX_RUNS (MAX_RUNS=5 now asserts one run). Validation: bash tests/regression_drain_stop_observability.sh.

title: Fix ORCA_BASE_REF propagation from start.sh to agent-loop.sh
description:
## Problem
`ORCA_BASE_REF` is honored by `setup-worktrees.sh`, but tmux-launched `agent-loop.sh` does not reliably receive it from `start.sh`. In testing, setting `ORCA_BASE_REF=does/not/exist` still resulted in run branches based on `main`.

## Scope
- Ensure `start.sh` injects `ORCA_BASE_REF` into each tmux session environment (same pattern as other runtime knobs).
- Add startup validation for `ORCA_BASE_REF` when set (clear error if ref is invalid/unresolvable).
- Keep direct `agent-loop.sh` behavior consistent with documented override semantics.
- Update docs where needed to describe override behavior accurately.

## Acceptance criteria
- With `ORCA_BASE_REF` set to a valid ref, run branches are created from that ref.
- With `ORCA_BASE_REF` set to an invalid ref, startup fails fast with clear error.
- Setup and run flows have consistent override behavior.
close_reason: Completed: ORCA_BASE_REF propagation + fail-fast validation implemented

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented ORCA_BASE_REF propagation from start.sh into tmux-launched agent-loop sessions and added fail-fast ORCA_BASE_REF commit-resolution validation in start.sh, setup-worktrees.sh, and direct agent-loop startup. Validated invalid ref failures in all three scripts.

title: Deprecate discovery-log feature while keeping follow-up issue creation
description:
## Problem
Discovery-specific machinery appears unused and adds prompt/schema/runtime complexity (discovery log paths, dedicated discovery protocol section, mandatory discovery summary fields), while follow-up issue creation itself remains useful.

## Scope
- Remove/deprecate discovery-log specific plumbing:
  - prompt placeholders and env vars dedicated only to discovery log files
  - discovery log file creation/touching if no longer needed
  - heavy discovery protocol wording in prompt/docs
- Keep follow-up issue creation guidance in queue workflow.
- Simplify run summary contract by making discovery-specific fields optional or removing them (with a compatibility strategy for existing metrics consumers).
- Update docs and validation logic consistently.

## Acceptance criteria
- Agents are still guided to create follow-up issues when needed.
- Discovery-log-only paths and instructions are removed.
- Summary/metrics schema and status tooling remain coherent after simplification.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented discovery-log deprecation across prompt/loop/docs, made discovery summary fields optional with compatibility validation, and passed regression_drain_stop_observability.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Completed and merged in 1acbb86: removed discovery-log-only prompt/env/runtime plumbing, made discovery summary fields optional with compatibility checks, and updated docs/test coverage (regression_drain_stop_observability passed).

title: Add orca status quick/full modes for operator ergonomics
description:
## Problem
`orca status` is expensive and noisy for frequent monitoring; operators need a lightweight view by default.

## Scope
- Add status modes/flags, e.g. `--quick` and `--full`.
- Define a minimal quick view (health summary, active sessions, latest agent activity, high-signal alerts).
- Keep detailed sections available behind full mode.

## Acceptance criteria
- Quick mode is substantially faster and focused on active operations.
- Full mode retains current deep diagnostic output.
- CLI help documents both modes and defaults.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented status modes with quick as default and full as legacy diagnostics. Added --quick/--full parsing + help in status.sh, preserved full output path behind --full, and updated orca.sh/README/OPERATOR_GUIDE docs. Validation: bash -n status.sh orca.sh; ./orca.sh status --quick; ./orca.sh status --full; timing check showed ~1.52s quick vs ~2.62s full in this run.

title: Implement mechanical planner v1 for agent->issue assignment
description:
Problem
- With default assignment mode, Orca needs a deterministic planner to pick issues per agent before launch.

Scope
- Add planner command/script (e.g. orca plan) that selects ready issues by priority and outputs assignments for N agents.
- Enforce metadata rules:
  - px:exclusive => only one assignment in the batch
  - ck:<key> conflicts => do not co-assign issues sharing contention keys
  - unlabeled issues are parallel-allowed
- Output machine-readable plan artifact for auditability.

Acceptance Criteria
- Planner is deterministic for same queue snapshot and inputs.
- Plan includes reason codes for skipped/held issues (exclusive/conflict/not-enough-slots).
- No runtime issue classification beyond labels and queue state.
close_reason: Completed: deterministic planner v1 implemented and integrated

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented planner v1 via new plan.sh and integrated start.sh assigned-mode launches to consume deterministic plan output. Planner enforces px:exclusive and ck:<key> contention, emits machine-readable artifacts under agent-logs/plans/YYYY/MM/DD/, and reports held reason codes (exclusive/conflict/not-enough-slots). Added orca plan command wiring, README/OPERATOR docs, and regression_planner_v1 test; validated with bash -n, regression_planner_v1, and regression_assigned_issue_contract.

title: Add Ubuntu/WSL bootstrap installer with br setup and fail-hard auth gating
description:
Problem
- Operators need a guided setup path that can install dependencies and clearly stop when manual intervention is required.
- We also need a consistent, official install path for `br` on Ubuntu/WSL.

External Reference (beads_rust)
- Upstream README install guidance recommends:
  - `curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust/main/install.sh?$(date +%s)" | bash`
- Upstream notes path precedence pitfalls:
  - install script uses `~/.local/bin`
  - `cargo install` uses `~/.cargo/bin`
  - mixed paths can result in wrong `br` version being invoked
- Installer supports `--verify` and installation destination options.

Scope
- Add `orca bootstrap` command for Ubuntu/WSL with guided, mostly-automated setup.
- Bootstrap should:
  1. install missing Ubuntu dependencies via apt (non-interactive mode supported, e.g. `--yes`)
  2. ensure `python` command availability via `python-is-python3` when needed
  3. install `br` via upstream install script (preferred path) and verify `br --version`
  4. normalize/verify PATH precedence so the intended `br` binary is active (`which br` + clear warning/fail on mismatch)
  5. initialize queue workspace when missing (`br init`)
  6. ensure queue id prefix is set (`br config set id.prefix orca`) if missing
  7. prompt to configure local git identity by default (repo-local `user.name`/`user.email`)
  8. check Codex availability/auth; fail hard with exact remediation steps when manual auth is still required
- Add `--dry-run` and clear step logging.

Potential Issues / Edge Cases to Handle
- `sudo`/apt permissions may be unavailable; fail with explicit instructions instead of partial setup ambiguity.
- Corporate proxies/offline systems can break curl-based installs; provide fallback guidance.
- PATH updates may not apply until shell restart; explicitly detect and message this.
- Avoid destructive changes to global git config by default; local config first.

Acceptance Criteria
- On a clean Ubuntu/WSL environment, `orca bootstrap --yes` can bring system to a state where `orca doctor` passes all local prerequisites except any unresolved manual auth.
- If manual auth (Codex login) is required, bootstrap fails hard with clear next commands.
- `br` is installed via the upstream recommended path and validated.
- Bootstrap behavior is deterministic and leaves clear logs of actions taken.

comments.author: assistant
comments.text:
Risk guidance for implementation:
- Avoid blind curl-to-bash usage for br installation. Prefer a pinned release/version and verify integrity (for example using installer checksum options) whenever feasible.
- Explicitly detect active br binary path and fail when path precedence is ambiguous or points to an unintended location. Mixed ~/.cargo/bin and ~/.local/bin is a common failure mode.
- Add an early, deterministic Codex auth probe so bootstrap can fail hard quickly with clear remediation commands when auth is missing.

title: Add machine-readable status output and follow-mode monitor stream
description:
Problem
- orca status is primarily human-readable text; machine consumers must scrape prose.
- There is no native follow-mode event stream for automation/monitoring agents.

Why this matters
- Agent-driven orchestration needs stable machine-readable observability contracts to reduce brittle parsing and operator babysitting.

Scope
- Add machine-readable status mode:
  - orca status --json
  - include stable schema/version and key fields needed by automation.
- Add follow-mode monitor command (or status follow extension) that emits structured JSON lines events for lifecycle transitions, for example:
  - session_started
  - run_started
  - run_completed
  - run_failed
  - loop_stopped
- Support session scoping options consistent with existing status behavior.
- Include health/alert signals in machine-readable form.
- Document schema and provide examples for downstream agent loops.

Implementation Notes / Potential Issues
- Preserve backward compatibility for existing human-readable status output.
- Prevent duplicate event spam in follow mode; define event identity/dedup strategy.
- Keep monitoring efficient on large metrics files (respect current status performance work).
- Handle missing/partial artifacts robustly during in-progress runs.

Acceptance Criteria
- status --json outputs parseable, stable machine-readable payload with explicit top-level schema version.
- follow-mode monitor emits structured events suitable for piping into automation.
- Session scoping works consistently across text/json/follow surfaces.
- Docs describe contracts and include at least one automation example consuming JSON output.

title: Prefer local main as default base ref in setup and run flows
description:
## Problem
Orca still has local-main precedence gaps: both worktree setup and per-run branch creation can prefer `origin/main`, which causes stale execution when local `main` is ahead.

## Desired behavior
For local Orca operation, local `main` is the default source of truth unless explicitly overridden.

## Scope
- Update base-ref selection in `setup-worktrees.sh` so default preference is:
  1. `ORCA_BASE_REF` (explicit override)
  2. local `main`
  3. `origin/main`
  4. current branch fallback
- Update run-branch base selection in `agent-loop.sh` to follow the same precedence.
- If both local `main` and `origin/main` exist and differ, emit an explicit warning with ahead/behind counts.
- Keep existing ability to force a ref through `ORCA_BASE_REF`.
- Update docs (`README.md`, `OPERATOR_GUIDE.md`) to match behavior.

## Acceptance criteria
- New worktrees default from local `main` when present.
- Per-run branches also default from local `main` when present.
- Divergence warning is visible when `main` and `origin/main` differ.
- Documentation matches implemented precedence across setup + run flows.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented base-ref precedence alignment in setup-worktrees.sh and agent-loop.sh: ORCA_BASE_REF -> main -> origin/main -> current branch; added divergence warnings with ahead/behind counts when main and origin/main differ; updated README.md and OPERATOR_GUIDE.md; validated with bash -n setup-worktrees.sh agent-loop.sh.

title: Propagate ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO and ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH from start.sh
description:
## Problem
Documentation says `ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO` and `ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH` are injected into agent runs, but `start.sh` does not pass them into tmux session env. Overrides are silently ignored.

## Scope
- Wire `ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO` and `ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH` through `start.sh` into per-session env, alongside existing helper vars.
- Add startup validation in `start.sh`:
  - `ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO` points to a valid git worktree.
  - `ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH` is executable.
- Ensure logs from `agent-loop.sh` reflect overridden values when set.
- Update docs only if behavior intentionally differs.

## Acceptance criteria
- Running `start.sh` with overrides results in agent-loop using overridden primary repo and lock helper.
- Invalid override values fail fast at startup with clear errors.
- README/operator docs are consistent with runtime behavior.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented in start.sh: pass ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO and ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH into tmux agent env, add startup validation for both overrides, and updated README startup checks. Validated with bash -n plus fail-fast checks for invalid repo path and non-executable lock helper.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Implemented in start.sh: propagate ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO and ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH into tmux-launched agent-loop env, and validate both overrides at startup. README startup checks updated; verified with bash -n and invalid override fail-fast runs.

comments.author: agent-1
comments.text: Completed and merged to main (1513ca8). start.sh now validates ORCA_PRIMARY_REPO as git worktree and ORCA_WITH_LOCK_PATH as executable, and injects both vars into tmux-launched agent-loop sessions. README startup validation list updated accordingly.
